data.title: Why Strait Of Hormuz Restrictions Could Have Far-Reaching Impact
data.description:
With movement in the Strait of Hormuz being heavily restricted by Iran, are investors underestimating the true scale of the disruption? Phil Davis, Founder of Philstockworld.com, breaks down why the impact could extend far beyond oil prices and how investors should be thinking about a prolonged period of uncertainty.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: As the conflict in the Middle East continues, a key focus for markets remain the Strait of Hormuz and whether it's open or closed. About 20% of the world's oil shipments travel through that area, and Iran is heavily restricting its use. And that, of course, has big implications for energy prices, inflation, and global growth.

On today's show, we're going to be looking at how investors could be positioning in this environment. And who better to provide some of that insight that one of our favorite guests, Phil Davis, founder of Philsstockworld.com.

Phil, it is always great to see you. How are you?

Phil Davis: I'm great. Kim, how are you doing?

Kim Parlee: I'm doing well. I'm doing well. We're happy to talk to you today. Always appreciate your thoughts on what's happening. And boy, is there are a lot happening.

I understand that you believe that the market right now is underestimating the real impact of the Strait of Hormuz crisis. Maybe so walk us through what you're seeing and why you feel that way.

Phil Davis: OK. Well, the thing you said right at the top-- you said 20 million barrels, and that's 20% of the world's supply. That is, I believe, an incorrect way of looking at it, and we hear it over and over and over again. But if you think about it, America, 13 million barrels a day. Canada, 5 million barrels a day. Mexico, a couple million barrels a day. Venezuela, 1.5 million barrels a day.

So, on our side of the world, we're producing 22, 23 million barrels of oil a day. So the global supply that we're talking about is less our oil. And that's a very, very big deal. You have to look at it that way, because they've only got 60 million barrels-- or, I'm sorry, 80 million barrels total left after we take out ours. We're not shipping it over there.

So, the amount of available oil in the area is 80. And, frankly, then you've got to take out China, and you've got to take out Russia's oil, which is-- Russia's oil is for Russia, China's oil for China, for the most part. So there's another 20 million barrels that's not available to the global supply.

That leaves 60 million barrels of total global supply, and 20 million-- which is a third-- is cut off from the Strait of Hormuz. So it's devastating for the countries that rely on that oil, like Japan and India and Germany-- major countries that are going to grind to a halt for lack of oil.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, no. Again, when you get out of, I'll call, the economic averages, so to speak, and start to focus on where it is, it's a big difference. You talked about Japan. You talked about India. What are you hearing about on the ground, what it's like for parts of Asia that are dealing with that severe restriction of oil?

Phil Davis: Yeah, they're physically constrained. Because the missing oil, it's not quite 20 million barrels a day because some is finding alternate routes and so on and so forth. But whatever oil is not getting out is permanently gone from the supply. You're basically taking the supply chain and you've cut or you've drained out, like we're doing with the Strategic Petroleum Reserves-- you're physically draining away any excess capacity, which makes you much tighter the next time there's some kind of an emergency. And this emergency is sure not over.

So, countries are physically having to shut down and conserve. Now, that's, to some extent, demand destruction-- which is, we know, another factor that we have to take into account down the road. But Japan is tapping their reserves. South Korea is tapping their reserves. And they have to actually physically shut down. Like, they're saying these planes can't run seven days a week. They have to shut down two days a week.

Kim Parlee: So, huge impact on parts of Asia, severe impact, I will say, on that. When you bring it back to then North America and Europe, is it much less so? Is it overstated then there? Or what are you seeing there?

Phil Davis: Well, the funny thing about America-- and you guys too, though. You export oil, you're a net exporter of oil. We're a net-- we import oil. We produce 13 million barrels, we import another 3 or 4 million barrels. But the funny thing is, we export 5 million barrels a day of refined products. So, a lot of what people think of as North American consumption or American consumption is actually just oil that we're taking to our refineries and turning it into products and then sending them out of the country. We're not actually consuming it.

The other thing is-- I'm sure you know what force majeure is. Force majeure is the ability to declare contracts voided because of circumstances like a war. So, in many parts of the world, they're declaring force majeure. The oil is undeliverable due to the war.

We don't have that. Canada and America can't say to Mexico, "We're declaring force majeure because of the war." The war's on the other side of the world. So we're less affected in that way than the Asian countries and the European countries that are having contracts canceled. And that also impacts their delivery prices because they have to go to the spot market, and the spot market in Asia is like $150 a barrel now.

Kim Parlee: What are you watching as you look ahead? There's a lot of headlines. It can be very confusing to people sometimes. It's all going badly. Sometimes it's all going well. And it changes minute by minute. What are you watching in terms of, I'll say-- I know you think that we're one drone strike away from some pretty bad things happening. I also know you're watching second-order effects. So let's just talk about those a bit.

Phil Davis: Well, the second-order effects-- if you go back to the strait, the second-order effect is that it's too risky to send ships through the strait. Therefore, the insurance companies are saying if you're even going to send a ship through, it's going to be astronomically expensive to insure it, or we won't insure it at all. And that effectively grounds the ship, because an oil-laden ship is effectively a bomb. And all it takes is one drone hitting the ship and the whole thing goes up. So you lose the shipment, you lose the ship, et cetera, et cetera. It's not worth the risk.

Not to mention the fact, where do you get the guys who want to get on that ship and crew it across the Strait of Hormuz? Are you going to really compensate them for the risk to their lives? They're not army guys. They're sailors.

So, there's knock-on effects like that. And then, oh, an interesting one is they've got the LNG tankers tied up in the Strait of Hormuz. They're not making their crossings like they're supposed to. And therefore, natural gas in part of Texas-- not the main terminal, but it's one of the subterminals in Texas-- is actually selling for negative money because they have nowhere to put it.

So they pay you to take away the natural gas. And meanwhile, if you could somehow magically get it over to Japan, they'll pay a fortune for it.

Kim Parlee: Yeah. Well, I guess this is where we're understanding the geographic importance of where resources are yet again. Listen, I've only got about 10 seconds here before I have to go to break. But just quickly, how do you think investors need to think about this environment?

Phil Davis: I think you have to embrace the uncertainty. And we've tremendously pared back to cash in our portfolios because it is too uncertain, it's too risky. You can't trust what you're hearing from the politicians. Everybody is telling a different story. It's just a nightmare to try to figure out what's actually happening.

data.title: AbbVie: Strong 2026 Outlook, Expect Dividend Increases
data.description:
Shares of AbbVie Inc. (ABBV) are down approximately 10% since my October 2025 update, in which I outlined the strong commercial progress and the company’s emerging efforts in the obesity market with the in-licensed amylin analog candidate GUBamy, now called ABBV-295. I was also cautious about the near-term share price performance expectations as the valuation reached 10-year highs, and that is what has essentially happened since then, although worsening market conditions did most of the work.

I am still not expecting miracles from AbbVie in the near term, but I continue to see it as well-positioned for long-term growth. I also expect dividend increases to accelerate in the following years as the company returns to stronger top- and bottom-line growth.

Expect Double-Digit Revenue Growth This Year With Skyrizi And Rinvoq Doing A Lot Of The Heavy Lifting Again

AbbVie had a strong end to 2025 with $61.16 billion in total revenue. This number came slightly ahead of the $61.1 billion midpoint of my revenue estimate range, which I have been increasing throughout the year.

Since my last update, we have seen more of the same, with Skyrizi and Rinvoq being responsible for most of the revenue upside versus expectations.

I expect to see more of the same this year, with both products delivering 20%+ revenue growth, driven by continued adoption in existing indications.

Management guided for $67 billion in revenue this year, a 9.5% increase over 2025, and I expect to see double-digit topline growth this year. My preliminary revenue estimate range for 2026 is $66.7-68.9 billion, with the $67.8 billion midpoint being $800 million ahead of AbbVie’s guidance.

The difference is coming from a $1 billion higher revenue estimate for the immunology segment (Skyrizi and Rinvoq) and slightly higher expectations for the neuroscience segment, offset by lower expectations for the oncology and aesthetics segments. Guidance for eye care and combined other products was not provided, but we can get it by calculating the difference to the sum of the largest segments, and I am in line with this combined difference.

I expect adjusted EPS in 2026 to be above $15 per share versus the company’s $14.37-14.57 guidance range, but this does not include estimates for IPR&D expenses that the company incurs throughout the year.

In the first quarter, AbbVie guided for $14.7 billion in total revenue, and I expect nearly $15 billion at the midpoint of my estimate range, with the same upside drivers (immunology and neuroscience) and downside drivers (oncology and aesthetics).

I expect adjusted EPS in the first quarter to be north of $3.05 versus the company’s $2.97-3.01 guidance range, driven by the expected revenue outperformance.

Expect Dividend Increases And More Business Development From Growing Revenues And Cash Flows

I have to give credit to AbbVie for increasing the dividend throughout the Humira erosion period. The quarterly dividend has increased from $1.41 at the start of 2022 to $1.73 at the start of 2026, a 5.4% dividend growth rate during this period.

With Humira erosion in the rearview mirror, the digestion of larger acquisitions from the last few years, and a strong top- and bottom-line growth outlook for 2026 and the following years, I expect to see acceleration in dividend increases in the following years.

AbbVie expects to generate $18.5 billion in free cash flow this year, and I expect business development activity to pick up, and I expect the obesity pipeline to be the main area the company will focus on.

Obesity Pipeline Needs Reinforcements - The Undifferentiated Amylin Analog Is Insufficient

AbbVie recently reported additional phase 1 results of ABBV-295 in obese patients. Weight loss ranged from 7.7% to 9.7% at week 12 when ABBV-295 was dosed weekly, 7.8% when dosed every other week, and 6.8% when it was dosed monthly. The results for the bi-weekly and monthly doses look better at week 13 with 9.7% and 7.9% reductions, respectively.

We are yet to see the safety data, as all we got was AbbVie saying that gastrointestinal side effects were the most common side effect and that they were mostly mild.

With the caveats of cross-trial comparisons, this puts ABBV-295 in the middle of the amylin pack, as it achieved better weight loss than Roche’s (RHHBY) and Zealand Pharma’s (ZLDPF) petrelintide, which had up to 8.6% weight loss at week 16 and which delivered disappointing weight loss data in the phase 2b trial recently, and ABBV-295 delivered worse weight loss than Eli Lilly’s eloralintide, which achieved 8.5% to 11.3% weight loss at week 12 (page 25 of the 2025 ADA presentation).

Every other week and monthly dosing looks achievable, but this is also unlikely to be a differentiating feature, as I expect Pfizer’s amylin analog, which it gained through last year’s acquisition of Metsera, to be competitive with eloralintide and ABBV-295 on efficacy and with ABBV-295 on dosing frequency with monthly and potentially less frequent dosing.

Both Pfizer and Eli Lilly, as well as Novo Nordisk (NVO), are better positioned than AbbVie at the moment, as they have plenty of other drug class candidates to combine with their amylin analogs, and Novo Nordisk already has a late-stage dual GLP-1/amylin agonist called amycretin (both subcutaneous and oral versions). In the phase 2 trial, amycretin delivered up to 22% weight loss without a plateau at week 36.

As such, a standalone amylin analog is unlikely to create significant shareholder value, especially for a company of AbbVie’s size. It is a good foundation molecule, but it needs combination partners.

I must say I expected more from AbbVie’s business development team, as it has been a year since the deal with Gubra, and time is not AbbVie’s friend here, as the field is evolving rapidly and combination strategies are critical to gain considerable market share. Yes, AbbVie can in-license or acquire additional assets, but it is losing time by not having candidates from other drug classes to combine with ABBV-295.

Conclusion

AbbVie is going through a share price consolidation phase, and I do not see much that is wrong with the company. Quite the contrary, I continue to see it as well-positioned for long-term value creation, and I expect to see healthy increases in the quarterly dividend in the following years.

The only meaningful criticism I have is the lack of urgency on the obesity side, as it has been a year since the foundational in-licensing deal with Gubra. I would like to see the company do more deals this year.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook - Stocks Reach New Lows As War Goes On
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Equities are feeling the intense heat of the swift return to risk aversion.

US stock benchmarks have dropped to new lows since the conflict began, with the Nasdaq and S&P 500 under the most severe pressure (while the Dow Jones is not there yet).

Across the board, major indices are down 1% and more for the session, with the pump-fake from Monday now turning against itself: Any hopes for a quick peace deal have proven short-lived, and the market's previous easing in sentiment was merely a timid pause.

Bulls who bought that dip are now paying the consequences.

The cause: A classic, violent rebound in energy markets: Oil continues to surge, with Brent crude pushing back above the critical $110 per barrel mark. This spike in energy costs is logically reawakening inflation fears and dragging down risk assets – But this time, gold is actually faring better.

The particular turn comes from the fact that traders are actively pricing in the risk of a severe escalation over the weekend: the impending arrival of a 4,500-strong Marine fleet in the Middle East is adding to the immense geopolitical anxiety.

Investors are tracking headlines by the minute, waiting to see if an anticipated Iranian counterproposal to a security deal actually materializes.

As traditional markets close, all eyes will turn to Bitcoin (BTC-USD) over the weekend. Because it trades 24/7, it will act as the primary real-time barometer for global sentiment.

Let's spot where today's rough price action is heading by looking at today’s intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

Today's selloff is surprisingly pulled by the largest cap stocks and isn't so widespread, with energy stocks naturally appreciating from the bounce in commodities, but utilities and industrials are somehow rising despite the broad pessimistic mood – A turn to defensives?

Among the laggards, some large-cap finance stocks are getting hammered, including JPMorgan (JPM), Visa (V), and Mastercard (MA), while virtually all the Mag 7s are also dropping, with Amazon (AMZN) being the worst performer of them all.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

DJIA has officially re-entered its bear channel after a failed attempt to break above – bulls could not hold the 4H 50-period MA, rejecting the major 46,600 intraday level signaled throughout our analysis from this week.

Now 300 points shy of its war lows, the index will face pressure ahead if the narrative doesn't get better, particularly with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq breaking their own lows.

A short-term target for the current selloff would be the lows of the channel around 44,500-44,600.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • 45,700 to 45,900 August Pivot
  • 46,300 intraday momentum resistance (4H 50-period MA)
  • Resistance 47,000 +/- 100 Points (session highs and major resistance)
  • Momentum Resistance 47,500 to 47,650
  • Key Resistance at 48,000
  • 48,400 to 48,500 mini-resistance

Support Levels

  • January 2025 Highs and War Lows 45,280
  • Channel lows 44,600
  • Next Minor Support 44,200 to 44,500
  • Major Support 43,500 to 43,750

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq is now testing a pivotal support that preceded the rally to its end-2025 records.

The ongoing selloff is a brutal one, with a measured move hinting at 23,000 if bulls fail to hold the current levels. Watch out for a gap lower to there depending on weekend narratives.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • August 2025 Pivot at 23,500 to 23,650
  • Major 2026 range lows 23,800 to 24,000
  • 24,450 to 25,550 resistance
  • Mini-intraday Resistance 24,750
  • Key Resistance 25,000 to 25,200 (Range highs – Long-term Bullish above)

Support Levels

  • September 2025 pivotal Support at 23,300 to 23,400
  • Session lows 23,290
  • Next Main Support at 23,000 higher timeframe support
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 2H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 now officially broke its prior war lows, extending within its major downward channel.

The next support is at 6,360 to 6,380; if bulls can't manage to show up significantly there, the index may be doomed for a harsher correction ahead (currently only -8.50% from its ATH)

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • 6,442 War Lows Current Pivot
  • 6,570 to 6,600 Double Bottom Pivot
  • 6,680 to 6,700 Mini-resistance
  • 6,740 Key intraday resistance
  • Pivotal Resistance 6,770 to 6,800

Support Levels

  • 6,360 to 6,380 Key August 2025 Support & Channel Lows
  • 6,300 psychological level
  • January 2025 ATH 6,152

data.title: The Only 'Superpower' Left In This Wall Of Worry Market
data.description:
Recently I was reading an article from Morgan Stanley (MS): “Who Is On The Other Side?” and it is an article rich in so many aspects that it is even difficult to summarize all the ideas.

But in this article, I will draw some parallels with the current market and with my own experiences applying some of these concepts, such as time arbitrage as a “superpower”.

A Word About Time Arbitrage

The Morgan Stanley article, specifically by authors Michael J. Mauboussin and Dan Callahan, draws on Treynor to explain what time arbitrage is and how it can be used by the investor to improve their returns.

The concept is quite simple to understand; basically, it is understood that an investor operating in different timeframes than other investors manages to have an edge. For instance, think if you are a hedge fund manager.

There is a gigantic pressure for you to beat your benchmark, which for stocks is generally the S&P 500 (SPY) (IVV). Even if the manager is not exactly a short term trader, he will still be affected by this pressure to choose the right investments but also to have them work out as quickly as possible. That is, you don’t want to buy a stock with a high margin of safety and have it stay sideways for 3 years, even if in the 4th year it compensates for all that waiting. As a manager who will be evaluated by customers not only against the benchmark but also against your peers, you want your total return to look “good” and better than the average in timeframes suitable for your strategy.

Since the market is short-termist, and many people choose their funds based on total return, often in short windows, such as 6 months and 1 year, this pressure is truly significant.

The investor base also matters for institutional investors; even if you believe in a stock, perhaps you have to sell at an inopportune moment just because a part of your investor base wants to withdraw their money at that moment.

Now, think if you are an individual investor who invests for the long-term. The game changes completely; you don’t need to give satisfaction regarding sizing or why stock x or y is underperforming. You don’t need to sell or buy an asset due to pressure, and therefore, you can take advantage of this “time arbitrage”.

Returning to the article, the example the authors use to illustrate this is excellent. It would basically be when the market is acting because of some noise and is missing the long-term signal. More specifically, the example used in the article is that, at some moments, a coin can fall 7 times in a row as tails out of 10 tosses, and therefore, the market prices this coin as “biased”.

But being a simple concept does not make it simple to apply. In fact, it is quite challenging and depends on other factors such as having access to information, having controlled emotions, having an environment that allows for this long-term hold, and so on. More specifically, the article mentions that there are 3 requirements for the investor to take advantage of time arbitrage: 1. Separate the signal from noise; 2. The signal needs to appear at some point, and 3. “Patient capital” is necessary so that it is possible to wait for that signal to appear.

And the data corroborates this view. It is not just something that can be verified empirically. Note in the chart below how the longer the horizon, the higher the monthly return.

Not surprisingly, they added a quote right below this chart (exhibit 15) mentioning how having this long term perspective is “the closest you can come to possessing an investing superpower”.

And I agree with that. Of course, as I mentioned, it is not as simple as “hold your stocks forever and beat the index”. You need to know how to use this time to be able to extract alpha from the cases in which the market is wrong. And often, this will be more linked to temperament than to some “unique” factor that you discovered alone while analyzing, especially because, even if information continues to be a must-have along with good analytical skills, today information is very widespread; that is, the information a fund manager has is probably equal to or greater than yours.

My Experience With Time Arbitrage

What I liked most about this concept and the article in general is that it ended up explaining a part of my strategy. Something I was already doing but didn't know how to define so well.

In fact, my first example will not be a stock where I had good returns, but rather something where I avoided bad returns. At the beginning of 2026, I wrote an article with a sell rating on Barrick (B). A few weeks after that article, I wrote another one with a sell rating on Newmont (NEM). The Barrick article you can check here, and the Newmont one check it here.

I received some fervent comments on these two articles, and I wasn't very blunt in my article. In fact, I believe they are very balanced articles where I cover the “what ifs” of gold, show that the margin of safety for these gold miners at the moment was not appealing, and therefore I was avoiding them. Even if it seemed “wrong” to be out of gold at the time, my opinion was, I'm going to avoid this asset; it looks crowded, and the market has assumptions like “central banks will buy gold forever”, etc.

Since then, both stocks have already accumulated a negative total return of ~-20%.

My point here was exactly what I said in the investing experts podcasts:

“my take was, I'm an individual investor, I'm not a hedge fund manager, so I don't think I have to beat the S&P (SP500) in the next quarter just in the next decade or five years so I'm not willing to bet that Barrick (B) and all these gold miners will be selling gold for $5k in the next year or two years, three years so I can be patient.”

But even after gold rose so much, it seemed obvious that this was the “right asset” to have in the portfolio. If a manager missed this rise with so many other funds invested in gold and with all of their customers knowing how “obvious” this thesis was, he would probably be criticized. No wonder, sometimes and for some cases in the industry, it may feel more comfortable to lose together instead of missing something obvious. Well, as I mentioned, I am an individual investor; I don’t need to prove to anyone whether or not I have gold, so I could just buy other assets while waiting for gold to reach a more attractive level.

I haven't redone all the calculations for Barrick and Newmont yet, but at this price I am much more neutral, and I don't think it's something to “be avoided” completely, just in case you're wondering what my opinion is now.

Moving from an asset that I avoided to an asset that I managed to make money through time arbitrage, I think Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) was one of the most evident; I first bought it ~17 months ago. GOOGL stock was at $164 (today it is at ~$280).

Before buying, I saw several opinions, podcasts with several managers, and there were those who were bullish because they thought the market was mispricing the company and those who were bearish, thinking this was a company that was going to lose in the “AI trade”.

But in general, the valuation below the average and below other tech companies showed that for the most part, the market was applying a discount to Alphabet, thinking that the core (Search) could be disrupted by LLMs.

But when you opened the earnings release from this time that I bought, what you saw was revenue growing 14% YoY (and Google Search specifically was growing 13%+), operating margin rising from 29% to 32% YoY.

And going deeper into the Alphabet case, you found a company that had tons of optionalities, had YouTube mispriced, Waymo as a strong optionality, Google Cloud growing a lot, and management with several positive points on how the business was adapting, creating Circle to Search, AI Overviews and scaling Gemini:

“In search, recent advancements, including AI Overviews, Circle to Search and new features in Lens are transforming the user experience, expanding what people can search for and how they search for it. This leads to users coming to search more often for more of their information needs, driving additional search queries. Just this week, AI Overview started rolling out to more than 100 new countries and territories. It will now reach more than 1 billion users on a monthly basis. [...]”

- Earnings Call Q2 2024

And when I did my calculations, even with pessimistic assumptions regarding Search growth, the valuation was still appealing.

That was when I initiated a position; nobody wanted to own Google, and the time horizon mismatch was obvious. I was not participating in any AI race, not in the sense that if Microsoft (MSFT) rose more than Google in the next quarters, I wouldn't be sad; I can hold this for the long-term until the market understands that the fear regarding AI was excessive and that the valuation was very good, and I still had Alphabet's execution and optionalities in my favor.

The Market Today: Perfect For This ‘Strategy’

Since I started following the US market, in fact, even before that, I saw Microsoft with that premium of a quality compounder for being basically a global digital infrastructure company. We are not talking about a small software company here that is going to die in the SaaSapocalypse; we are talking about the company that, if this trend occurs, is the one that will probably swallow several software sub-niches because it already has the customers and the necessary infrastructure.

If we are talking about core Microsoft products, it is a sticky subscription that every company likely needs to have and will continue to have, in my view. This comes with new AI-related products that are already gaining traction and a Cloud division that is growing almost 40% a year.

The demand for this cloud division is also so high that the company has very high estimates for CapEx, so that it can meet this demand and continue growing at an accelerated pace and be one of the main digital infrastructure, cloud, AI, and token companies in the world, which are industries with very interesting prospects.

I even understand having a bit of apprehension because the scenario really isn't 100% clear for Microsoft. There is the whole issue with OpenAI (OPENAI) which makes it difficult to know if it's a moat or a liability. The company will still need to prove that the infrastructure expansion will have a positive ROIC and so on.

But such a large discount doesn't seem to make any sense to me. Today the company trades with a forward P/E of 21.8x and the market still estimates that the EPS will grow in the double-digits for the next 5 years (or more).

On the other side we have Walmart (WMT). A solid company, which is probably among the 10 most reliable in the US or in the world. It seems like the perfect place to hide from US inflation, potential geopolitical conflicts, and even an AI bubble.

And it truly would be the perfect place if Walmart were not a market consensus favorite. Estimates suggest a growth of ~5% per year for the top line. The EPS should grow something close to double-digit (lower than Microsoft) and that's counting on buybacks. The problem? The forward P/E is practically twice as large as Microsoft's.

Let's assume a scenario where Microsoft does not disappear and maintains reasonable prospects, in line with its execution history. Should Microsoft's return be ~2x higher than Walmart's? In fact, it should be even more, because, as I said, the growth prospects for Microsoft are better.

The market accepts having a very compressed total return, with an earnings yield of less than 3% for the next few years, because Walmart is much 'safer' than Microsoft.

Note how this looks in the chart below.

These two names were just the examples that came to my mind to explain the market right now. It’s not just these two names; it’s a more general trend.

Note how the S&P500 fell -3.4%, but sectors have very distinct performances. Utilities (XLU) did well, Consumer Staples (XLP) also did well, Energy (XLE) was a total outlier because of unexpected conflicts, while financial (XLF) underperformed a lot, and the Magnificent Seven (MAGS) was even worse, falling -10% in about 3 months.

I don't think a correction of ~10% in 3 months is the end of the world; it’s part of the game and is even healthy. My point here is to show you that the market is acting in a way that opens space for individual investors to use time arbitrage to their advantage.

There are still opportunities in utilities and consumer staples; even in energy, there must be. But I'm not going to get FOMO by having a small part of oil in my portfolio and buying now at the peak, or think that since volatility is very high, I'll go to a company like Walmart to protect myself.

What I am doing now is choosing names that I like, feel comfortable with in the long-term case and the valuation, like Amazon (AMZN) and others I mentioned here. In fact, something that I think should be mentioned is that at current levels, it seems much riskier to me to have Walmart than Microsoft. Returning to the article, there is a passage where they mention that sentiment ends up making the market underestimate the power of regression toward the mean:

“Sentiment can also lead to underestimating the power of regression toward the mean, which says that results that are far from average will be followed by results with an expected value closer to the average”

This seems to fit very well with the current stock market. Microsoft might be in a cloudier scenario, but is it really that uncertain and bad? Should the P/E trade so far below the average? Likewise, Walmart is safer, but is it that much safer than the average? Should the P/E be traded so far from the mean?

In short, the market is not efficient all the time, and the long-term ends up being a superpower for those who know how to use it (along with several other factors and requirements). Especially when we see so many people curious about the next quarter, knowing how to wait for the coming years ends up being a competitive advantage.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Market Shifts To Defense
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

It's been a macro week for the market (0:20) Defensive shift; AI stocks really suffering (0:55) Circle down (3:30) Jobs data will provide major catalyst (4:55) Fed and interest rates (7:20)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, welcome back to another round of Wall Street Roundup. Welcome back to the show after a week off. Talk to us, what are you thinking about these days?

Brian Stewart: The past week has really been a macro week for the market. There's just not a lot of corporate specific data coming out, earnings season's over. We're just not getting a lot of news flow. So really the market is moving on the big issues and the biggest issue obviously this week has been the conflict in Iran.

There were some signs early on, the Trump administration had come out and said that they were close to an end that negotiations were going on and then Iran pushed back on that. You kind of had the he said, he said process going on throughout the week.

The market is in the middle of based on that sort of defensive shift. So not surprisingly, you saw oil majors do very well this week. Exxon Mobil (XOM) was up 9%. Chevron (CVX) was up 7%.

Both of them are up about 40 % year to date, extending highs. Actually with ExxonMobil kind of leading the news or being kind of top of mind this week, I was getting a little nostalgic because when I was a kid, ExxonMobil was the biggest company in the world by market cap.

And so I just was thinking how interesting it is, how much the economy has changed over 40 years or so, where now, you know, it's sort of an also a rant on the list of biggest companies in the world. So it's kind of interesting to have those kind of stocks kind of in the news.

Again, another example of that is telecom stocks did very well this week. I mean, in a down week, AT&T (T) was up 3%, T-Mobile (TMUS) was up about 3%, Verizon (VZ) was up about 2%.

These are companies that are considered defensive stocks. They're cashflow stocks. They're dividend stocks, those companies has two to 6 % dividend yield.

Meanwhile, on the other side of the spectrum, you see the AI stocks really suffering in the past weeks. You see Micron (MU) is down 15%, Meta (META) is down 12, Oracle (ORCL) down eight, Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) down seven.

So you're seeing money kind of being pulled out of what has been the drivers of the last several years, the AI stocks. You see that money being reinvested in these kind of old economy kind of options.

Rena Sherbill: We had Victor Durgunov on Investing Experts. The episode was released yesterday. We talked on Wednesday. And he was talking about Schlumberger (SLB), which is the second episode in a row that somebody has touted so heavily, George Noble being the first.

And to your point, one of his reasons was its focus on AI and the future. And as this kind of past no name, now it's making its building for the future and also benefiting from this. The more things change, the more they stay the same type of trend that's happening now with everything that's going on geopolitically and the conflict and in the markets and in energy and these oil majors.

So interesting to note. What else are you thinking about this week? What else are you focused on?

Brian Stewart: The other newsy sector that popped up was Circle (CRCL) was down 20%. This was on reports that the Clarity Act, that's the legislation kind of making its way through Congress that would provide a little bit more regulation structure around crypto. That included severe limits on the amount of yields that stablecoins could have. So Circle dropped sharply on those reports.

It also pulled down Coinbase (COIN) was down 19 % in the past five days. It didn't drop as dramatically in the single day, but has moved lower pretty substantially through the week. So that's sort of on the back burner, like that those headlines are, I think, being buried by obvious people are concerned about Iran.

People are also concerned about DHS and the effect that TSA is having on air travel, things like that. And so there are other kind of sector related news items that are kind of bubbling up.

Rena Sherbill: Earnings wise, do you have anything to look ahead to?

Brian Stewart: Earnings are pretty dead again next week. Nike (NKE), I think, is the biggest highlight. We'll get some information from them about the state of consumer.

So even if you're not a Nike investor, I think there's some value in checking out some of the commentary around that. Meanwhile, I think the economic news, obviously more Iran headlines, I think, are going to be the dominant force next week as well.

But if we're looking at the information, the data that's about to come out, I think the jobs data on Friday provides a major catalyst. The last jobs report was very worrisome. The payrolls were down 92,000. Unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4%.

It's being described as a low hire, low fire environment. There's a lot of sort of ins and outs in the jobs data that's been coming out. So for instance a drop of 92,000 payrolls is kind of recessionary territory. However, 4.4 % unemployment rate isn't particularly worrisome.

However, however, like extra however, you have the labor participation rate is trending lower. It's about 62%. Now that's the number of people in the overall economy who are even looking for jobs or concerned about having a job.

So we saw this in the Great Recession and the financial crisis is after we started recovering from that, a lot of people just didn't go back to work. So you have situations where certain structural employment situations will drive people, say, into early retirement or send people back to school, or people will decide to be a one-income household or something along those lines.

And so you're seeing some signs of that. I think that might be early signs of the AI structural revolution that we've talked about a little bit. Like if companies are going to need fewer people to do jobs, you might see a situation where retirement comes early for some people, especially in an environment where an older worker might not have the same sort of innovative chops that you might need to operate with AI.

The kinds of experience that they have might be more outdated than somebody who's just coming out of school. You might see things like that. So it'll be very interesting to see what the new jobs report says.

Also, keep an eye on revisions. Usually, recently, the pattern has been for downward revisions. In the previous report losing 92,000 jobs, seeing whether or not that's actually worse than it was previously reported, whether those are gonna be a bounce back, whether we're gonna see more job growth or whether we're gonna see further deterioration there. I think that'll be interesting.

And then the effect on expectations for the Fed is another thing that I kind of have circled.

So with the hot inflation data that's been coming out and with the anticipation of higher fuel prices fueling more inflation, you had a major shift in the expectation for the market. So now the market is pricing and the possibility of higher interest rates.

Previously, lower interest rates had been sort of the norm. So at the next meeting, the one that's taking place in April, you have a 96% chance of no change, which is pretty much on target with where it was coming out of the last Fed meeting. But now there's a 4% chance of a rate hike when previously the sort of fractional probability was for a rate cut.

The hope as we started the year was that the Fed would be able to lower rates as we go and there's been a real shift lately. So going back to even just beginning of March, you had an 8% chance at the end of the year that rates would be the same as they are right now 0% chance of higher rates and a 10% chance that rates would be at least a full percentage point lower. So that would be four or more quarter point cuts between now and the end of the year.

And that's completely reversed. So now we're looking at a 61% chance of no change by the end of the year and a 39% chance of higher rates. So in the past three weeks or so, we've completely reversed the expectation for what's going to happen next in terms of interest rates.

Rena Sherbill: I would also point to that conversation with Victor. He gives a few minutes about the Fed and I find it very interesting. If you may not find it compelling, it's certainly interesting. I think it's compelling too, but you know, that's what makes horse races.

As we close out this conversation, as we close out March, any final notes about the market as we head into the weekend? As we're talking right now, the markets are down, treasury yields are up, hitting multi-month highs. Anything to note there, say?

Brian Stewart: One pitch I would make in terms of the Fed being compelling, interesting, but not compelling. Like if I was recommending the Fed as a TV show, I would also point out that there's the personality part with Powell and Trump and the incoming Fed chief who still needs Senate approval.

And so you had Powell say that he'll stick with the Fed because he'll be governor even after he's removed as, even after he's replaced as chairman. He's going to stick with the Fed until these investigations have reached a conclusion.

He's also said that he'll remain interim chairman, that he's willing to remain interim chairman as is the policy if we're still waiting for the new Fed chief. So there's a personality part of it that also makes it very interesting. Trump's obviously been pushing very hard for lower rates.

And so now you have a situation where the market is predicting higher rates. It'll be interesting to see what Fed's handpicked, or Trump's handpicked Fed chief will do once he's in there. In terms of the overall market and what to expect, I really think we're gonna be headline driven.

The decline that we've had recently, you have the S&P 500 (SP500) getting to levels it hasn't seen since September, you have the NASDAQ (QQQ) officially in correction territory dropping 10 % or more from its highs that overhang could disappear pretty quickly if the Iran conflict comes to what the markets perceive as a positive conclusion, especially a quick conclusion that allows oil prices to come down pretty sharply.

But if it begins to become clear that that's going to be a long term, that oil prices are going to be higher for the foreseeable future with the other inflationary pressures, with the necessity of the Fed to raise interest rates in the face of those inflationary pressures, even with a weakening or at least concerning labor market, there's a lot of headwinds being made.

So I think that the market's going to do its best to see the future in an environment where the future is very hard to see.

Rena Sherbill: Thank you for that. And it's interesting, funny that you called it a personality driven, I mean, on point too, in terms of the Fed, but the conversation was mostly predicated on me asking Victor what he would do if he were nominated instead of Warsh. So to your point that it's a very subjective affair these days, I would say.

Brian Stewart: What did he say? I didn't hear the interview.

Rena Sherbill: The first point that he was making is the connection between gold prices and interest rates and the way that the conversation has turned with interest rates makes it more compelling for the long term case for gold and gold miners.

Brian Stewart: I do think trading gold has been interesting lately because it hasn't been quite what one would expect in a situation where you've a spike in concern about geopolitical situation. I mean, that's usually kind of a gold hoarding event and you've had not quite that reaction in the market. So I think that sounds really interesting.

data.title: Draganfly's Make-Or-Break Moment: Revenues Must Ramp Up Now
data.description:
I have covered Draganfly Inc. (DPRO) before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail and explained why I considered it an interesting buy. Since then, the stock has dipped around 30%, especially this week after disappointing Q4 results. While the company has achieved some contracts, these have not yet translated into better results.

Background

Draganfly is a Canadian UAV company focused on hardware, software, sensors, and services mainly for defense, public safety, and industrial customers. In the last couple of years, its product line has evolved from high-payload multirotors for logistic operations and ISR platforms into a broader portfolio that now also includes first-person view (FPV) drones, which are now the strategic focus.

The Flex FPV is a modular platform that can be configured to suit the characteristics of various missions with different warheads, communications, and flight profiles. This includes one-way attack roles, as well as configurations thought for reutilizing the drone.

Draganfly has already secured military orders for several of its drones, such as its Commander 3XL, to be used for logistics within various branches of the Department of Defense, or for its Flex FPV. However, the company doesn’t provide the contract size, and given the limited revenue increase, I assume that those are small contracts.

Draganfly also participated in the first phase of Gauntlet I, the Pentagon’s Drone Dominance Program, with its FPV solution. However, it was not selected as one of the eleven winners. In the Q4 conference call, management commented that they had only a few days to prepare and hoped to be in the second phase. Given that they didn’t classify in the first round, I am not particularly optimistic about their possibilities in the second one.

Besides that, this year the company expects to achieve several contracts with Canada and is doing demonstrations with the military.

To meet the expected increase in demand, back in Q3 management spent a lot of time discussing a huge ramp in production capacity, with a recent increase to C$100 million and longer-term plans to multiply it by four by adding several new plants. However, in the Q4 conference call, management didn’t comment on it. I expect they are continuing with the plan, but the change in the tone is somewhat concerning.

​

Financial Position And Valuation

After several offerings, Draganfly has a strong financial position with around $66 million in cash and essentially no debt at the end of Q4. However, in February the company raised $50 million more, increasing its cash to more than $110 million.

Until its revenues increase above $50 million per year, which I expect in 2027 if they achieve the expected contracts, the company burns a meaningful amount of cash every quarter.

After all the talk in Q3 about the new plant that increased its capacity to C$100 million, I was expecting to see a stronger ramp in sales already in Q4. However, that did not happen, and Q4 revenues were lower than Q3 due to a reduction in service revenues. This has reduced my confidence in the company, and I am in show-me mode now. I want to see contracts and pipeline turning into revenue growth before believing long-term growth talk.

Despite this distrust, I believe that the company is really cheap with a market cap of $160 million when it has more than $110 million in cash. This is why, despite my frustration with Q4, I still see the valuation as interesting.

I don’t want to provide a fair value since they have to prove themselves, but if in the next few quarters the company really begins to grow its revenue, I wouldn’t be surprised to see the company be a multibagger from here.

One point that I see as clearly negative and increases my distrust about the company is the low ownership. Insiders own less than 1% of the company; I would prefer if the alignment was higher.

Risks

The main risk is that the inflection in revenue and cash flow simply does not arrive and that the company will continue to dilute shareholders to operate. Moreover, management is talking about continued investment and scaling of operations. I believe that market patience will run out this year, and if they don’t start to increase revenues, it wouldn’t surprise me to see the stock trading below its cash.

A second risk is the elevated competition in the sector. Gauntlet I was a clear example where there were 25 participants and only 11 got initial delivery orders. Even worse, many companies even didn’t score. Draganfly scored points but was not classified this time, although the company is positive about the next round.

Finally, as commented above, governance and alignment remain weak.

Conclusion

Draganfly is in the right sector at the right time, but it must seize the opportunity to deliver strong shareholder returns. For now, and despite all the promises, it has not been the case, and I believe the market is becoming impatient, as shown by the recent price action.

Q4 did not show the sales ramp I was hoping for. If this does not materialize in the next few quarters, I believe that the market will penalize the stock. However, while I am not willing to increase my position until they show the results, I believe that Draganfly can deliver.

data.title: Telco Turnaround: Can The Sector Still Recover Amid Price Pressures?
data.description:
There are signs of price pressures returning in the telecom space, but Vince Valentini, Managing Director for Equity Research with TD Cowen says some names in the space are still in a good position outside their core business.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Amid signs of price pressures returning to the Canadian telecoms sector, our featured guest today says there are still some positives beyond the core business concerns. Joining us now to discuss is Vince Valentini, managing director for equity research with TD Cowen.

Vince, great to have you back on the program. What is going on with telecoms? It's clearly not the headline story in all the financial press right now, but there's some interesting moves.

Vince Valentini: Yeah, and always great to be here, Greg. So, the stocks have held in reasonably well this year. I think some people are a bit concerned about some of the macro and geopolitical events going on. And this is somewhat of a safe haven sector.

There's the odd dusting of concern about a recession in Canada at some point. And again, these would be names you typically want to own in that type of environment. So I think that's keeping the fund flow to keep these names doing reasonably well.

But underneath the surface, we're getting a little bit worried that some of that price war stuff that we saw for pretty much two years straight, from the middle of 2023 to early 2025, all of which, as you recall, came after Rogers (RCI) acquired Shaw and was forced to sell their wireless business to Quebecor (QBCRF). That's when things started to get a bit more painful for the industry.

It seems to be coming back. The past two months even have seen much more aggressive pricing competition than we're used to seeing, especially at this time of the year when January, February, and March are typically slow seasonal months. If you're going to see the odd promotional misstep, it's going to be at back-to-school period or Black Friday period, not in the middle of February, when people are generally not shopping for new cell phone plans.

So, I would term it as we're on a bit of thin ice right now. We have the typical end-of-quarter games that get played by the telcos to try to jam a last few number of subscribers in that they can report when they report to us analysts in five or six weeks.

We need to see this behavior stop come April. If we continue to see what we see-- and I'll just give you one example, and good for consumers. If you're online there, thinking of the consumer side of telecom versus the investment side, go out and shop for a new wireless plan.

Virtually every Rogers, Bell (BCE), and Telus (TU) store right now has at least $35 for a hundred gigs of data. That typically should be $55 or $60. And there's even been sporadic sort of promos at $27.50 for 60 or 80 gigs of data. And a lot of times, they're throwing in US and Mexico roaming into these plans.

This is dramatically lower than where the price points need to be to get revenue stabilization for these companies. And it creates the risk that the "disrupter in the space," which is Quebecor and their Freedom Mobile brand, who actually are not the lowest-priced offer out there right now if you went to any mall and looked at the stores-- they may fire their own shot back. I think they'll give the carriers through the end of this quarter to see the end-of-quarter games.

But if we're sitting here in the second week of April and the big three telcos have not gotten more disciplined with their pricing, I would think Quebecor will escalate this price war even more. So I'd say the core business is on pretty thin ice right now, unfortunately. We need to see behavior turn quick, or else the investment community, I think, is going to get more concerned.

On top of the pricing-- the pricing is the more important dynamic and the one that's changing-- you obviously still have a backdrop of no real population growth. In fact, StatsCan data show we actually had negative population growth in the fourth quarter for the first time in a long, long time.

So, if you have no population growth driving the volume side of the equation, you're even more desperate to have stabilization in the price side of the equation. So, core wireless fundamentals are a bit challenged, as you said in your opening, which means we have to pivot and look for other ways to try to make money on these stocks.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about the other ways because each company, as you were pointing out to us before we got on the show, has its own unique-- everyone's got the core telecom business, and then they have a little something else that investors can hone in on. Maybe we'll take them company by company. Rogers.

Vince Valentini: Yeah, and Rogers, I think, is probably the biggest in terms of that because of their sports file. And they've had material interest in sports teams for over a decade, but it's become much bigger as a percentage of their market cap and enterprise value. And it's become much more relevant to us analysts and the investors because they're articulating a path to finally monetizing some of the teams and showcasing what the value is.

It's tough to showcase the value when the only person who's bought these teams in the past has been Rogers. It's their own money going in to buy the stake from BCE a couple of years ago, and their own money going up to buy Larry Tanenbaum's 25% stake, which is a deal we expect to happen later this year.

You need a proof point from a third party coming in and saying, yes, we agree with Rogers. This collection of teams, which is one of the biggest, most attractive collection of sports teams in North America-- as a reminder, Leafs, Raptors, and Blue Jays being the three core tenants, but they own other properties as well. That combined asset mix is worth in the range of $20 billion, but you need third-party capital to come in and give verification of that.

And it seems likely, according to what management is saying and our own analysis would back it up, that we will get that transaction sometime in 2026-- probably not till the fourth quarter. They have to deal with buying out Larry Tanenbaum's 25% stake first, which is a process that's going to run from July until September. Once they get that cleaned up, then they will immediately turn their attention to finalizing a deal with a consortium of wealthy individuals, private equity firms, and sell a stake. So that is still a path-- and we've published a lot on this recently-- a path to value creation for Rogers shareholders that's not fully or properly reflected in the share price yet, and not that far off if it's happening this year.

To be clear, we do expect a second transaction we've published on, but that's not happening this year. It may not even happen in 2027. It could be a 2028 event, where we see full separation of Rogers into a cable, wireless-focused entity, and then all of the sports, entertainment, and media assets in a different publicly traded vehicle, which we think is the best solution for the long-term value.

Because investors have a really tough time saying, how do we value a baseball team or a hockey team that doesn't generate any cash flow when it's buried within a boring, low-growth, but cash-generative cable and wireless business?

So you need to have these things separate so investors can value them properly. So we'd see that as the ultimate end game. So Rogers, for sure, tops the list, Greg, of companies that have something they can offer as upside to shareholders even if you're a bit concerned about that core wireless business not having much growth in the near term.

Greg Bonnell: Alright, let's get to the other big player, one of the other big players in the space, BCE. What do they have to offer beyond the core business?

Vince Valentini: I'd say it's still pretty big for them. It's not so much of a catalyst of a hidden asset that people can't value properly today that will explode once a transaction happens, the way you kind of have with Rogers in the sports. But if you add up all of their "non-core telecom stuff," you have their Ziply business in the US, which was their $7 billion acquisition just about two years ago.

It's still a telecom business, but it has no exposure to the Canadian market and the Canadian price war and wireless that may be starting to happen again. And the fiber business in the US is showing good signs of steady progress. As you build fiber-- it's the better technology-- you definitely win market share. So that's a good sort of mid-teens revenue and EBITDA growth business for them that's differentiated.

And then they have data centers, which, again, still has the tenets of a telecom business. You invest CapEx. Then once you own that infrastructure, you get recurring cash flow for many years to come. So not that much different than building a fiber or wireless network, but obviously, a bit nuanced that it's serving big enterprise customers and serving AI capabilities and AI compute.

And Bell has been somewhat hitting the cover off the ball on that strategy. It only started about a year ago when they first started talking about it. They had their first facility up and running in Q2 of last year, which was already fully pre-sold, and they got some nice revenue from it.

They gave some nice targets at their investor day in October as to what they're going to do with this business over the next three years. Those targets were to do 75 megawatts of computing power across six or seven data centers in Canada over the course of three years.

Just a little over a week ago, they announced one facility in Saskatchewan already fully powered, already fully partnered with in terms of customers and government relationships that are necessary to build it. 300 megawatts of capacity in one facility. So they're blowing through their targets that they had set. So it's starting to become a bigger business for them.

I don't want to overplay it. Even at really good success, it's maybe a billion dollars of EBITDA for Bell a few years down the road. So it's still sort of 10% of what the total company does. So, it's not going to become a pure-play data center, AI-processing company. But that's a nice adjunct to the core wireless and wireline business that you get a bit of differentiation with BCE as well.

data.title: Gold Bounces Despite The Oil Rally, A First Since The U.S.-Iran War
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The progressive explosion in crude prices, driven by failed hopes of a resolution to the Middle East conflict, has sent global assets struggling.

Investors and traders have been wondering how gold, as a safe haven and a traditional hedge against inflation, has been struggling so much throughout the entire month with bombs thrown around the Gulf.

Metals were actually large victims of their own prior success – After consistently rallying since October 2024, the asset class burnt its own wings.

Heavily chased commodities can see brutal swings when demand dampens: as money flows rushed into crude and the fundamental backdrop turned more hawkish, investors turned away from the shiny metals.

With most asset managers already long the asset class, as expressed in the February BofA Survey, no one was there to withhold the pressure.

But after a 25% correction in gold, we could see the bullion actually dominate its peers in the coming days – So why now?

A few technical patterns are providing some demand for it, like the 200-day MA acting as support (see below). Still, more importantly, gold is the only asset rallying while stocks, bonds, and other metals like silver and platinum are tumbling.

It has been a rough stretch for metals, but gold could rise from the ashes, particularly if investors start to price in further escalation (as seen with oil rallying back to $98 today).

After large corrections, it wouldn't be surprising to see the ultimate safe haven gain back some demand with the global economy projected to struggle.

Let's attack a high-level intraday analysis of gold (XAUUSD:CUR) as it attempts to break its curse amid the ongoing war.

Multi-timeframe analysis for Gold, starting from the Weekly to intraday

Gold Weekly timeframe

It would definitely not be crazy to assume that the correction for gold has come a long way, particularly as the selling erased most of the extension the metal did in 2026.

Weekly RSI is now back to neutral, allowing future movement to flow with less resistance.

Tumbling close to 25% to $4,100 lows, the Bullion rejected its support in an attempt to regain its major momentum pivot – Also forming a dragonfly doji (more bullish than bearish, but also a sign of hesitancy).

  • Breaking above $4,500 and closing there on the daily could easily relaunch the commodity towards $5,000 – with the current state of markets, that would mostly happen on a slow grind higher.
  • But with dojis, one also has to consider the downside – A daily close below the 50-week MA of $3,965 opens the door for a larger correction to $3,500 (less probable for now).

Gold Daily Chart

Looking closer, gold did find significant support at its 200-day MA, and after its previous day's failed dip below the key $4,400 level, bulls are now taking the advantage, particularly after a daily bullish RSI divergence.

Momentum still seems hesitant, and it will stay the same as long as prices remain within the $4,300 to $4,500 range – Any daily close above $4,550 (December 2025 record) would launch the commodity higher.

After the large correction, however, it would be difficult to see it suddenly explode (as said before, a progressive grind higher would make more technical sense).

Levels of interest for gold trading:

Resistance Levels:

  • $4,550 December 2025 record (intraday resistance)
  • February Wick Pivot $4,675 - $4,725 (bullish above)
  • $4,850 to $4,900 Key Resistance
  • $5,100 Pivotal Resistance
  • $5,400 mini-resistance

Support Levels:

  • Pivotal Support $4,355 – $4,400
  • Main Channel Lows Support & 200-Day MA $4,100
  • Next Support $3,880 to $4,000
  • $3,200 to $3,500 Major Support

Gold 2H Chart

Gold is getting back to a more bullish momentum from seller exhaustion – currently contained by its 2H 50-period MA on the intraday, bulls will want to push above in order to materialize a more significant rebound.

Keep a close eye on the triangle formation for upward and downward breakout boundaries – when markets reopen next week, traders can expect significant movement; hence, watch out for your size and orders!
